ocr.text: In a comparative analysis of clinical findings, Group I (254 visits, 118 patients) exhibited 76 (29.9) cases of Rash, 32 (12.6)
Gottron's papules, 1 (0.4) Ulceration, 28 (11.0) Nailfold changes, and 19 (7.5) Calcinosis; Group II (104 visits, 70 patients)
showed 24 (23.1) Rash, 11 (10.6) Gottron's papules, 1 (1.0) Ulceration, 9 (8.7) Nailfold changes, and 6 (5.8) Calcinosis; Group
III (307 visits, 131 patients) had 202 (65.8) Rash, 123 (40.1) Gottron's papules, 9 (2.9) Ulceration, 108 (35.2) Nailfold changes,
and 60 (19.5) Calcinosis; and Group IV (208 visits, 127 patients) recorded 159 (76.4) Rash, 117 (56.3) Gottron's papules, 26
(12.5) Ulceration, 100 (48.1) Nailfold changes, and 36 (17.3) Calcinosis. Statistical comparisons revealed that Group I vs.
Group II had 1.71 (NS) for Rash, 0.29 (NS) for Gottron's papules, 0.43 (NS) for Ulceration, 0.45 (NS) for Nailfold changes, and
0.33 (NS) for Calcinosis; Group I vs. Group III showed 71.57 (<0.0001) for Rash, 52.44 (<0.0001) for Gottron's papules, 5.11
(0.024) for Ulceration, 44.16 (<0.0001) for Nailfold changes, and 16.72 (0.0009) for Calcinosis; Group II vs. Group III had
57.28 (<0.0001) for Rash, 30.74 (<0.0001) for Gottron's papules, 1.27 (NS) for Ulceration, 26.84 (<0.0001) for Nailfold
changes, and 10.93 (0.0009) for Calcinosis. Furthermore, IL-1β findings indicated it is acutely upregulated after TBI in clinical
settings, specifically through cerebral microdialysis; adult and pediatric patient CSF, referenced by [100, 102, 116, 117].
Rash  Gottron's papules  Ulceration  Nailfold changes  Calcinosis
χ2 (P)
Group I vs. group II  1.71 (NS)  0.29 (NS)  0.43 (NS)  0.45 (NS)  0.33 (NS)
IL-1β
Finding  Clinical/experimental  Experimental setting  Reference
Acutely upregulated after TBI  Clinical  Cerebral microdialysis; adult and pediatric patient CSF  [100, 102, 116, 117]
Peripheral administration after TBI results in larger lesions and impaired behavioural outcomes  Experimental (rat)  Fluid percussion injury  [118]
Expression exacerbated and prolonged by secondary insult  Experimental (rat)  Diffuse axonal injury with posttraumatic hypoxia  [27]
Causes BBB dysfunction in vivo  Experimental (rat; in vitro)  Cerebral endothelial cells  [119]
Additionally, peripheral administration after TBI results in larger lesions and impaired behavioral outcomes in experimental
(rat) settings, particularly through fluid percussion injury as noted in [118]. The expression of IL-1β is exacerbated and
prolonged by secondary insult in experimental (rat) scenarios involving diffuse axonal injury with posttraumatic hypoxia,
referenced by [27]. Lastly, it causes BBB dysfunction in vivo in both experimental (rat; in vitro) contexts involving cerebral
endothelial cells, as cited by [119].
